characters.name: Bishop Saint (St. Nicholas or St. Martin)

event: The Solothurn Madonna (Virgin and Child with Saints)
symbols: crowned Virgin enthroned — Mary as Queen of Heaven, honored above all saints
bishop with crosier — the pastoral authority of the Church protecting the faithful
knight in armor with red banner — the Church Militant, faith defended by spiritual warfare
Christ child on Mary's lap — God incarnate, the source of all authority held by the flanking saints
description: An arched panel showing the Virgin Mary seated centrally, crowned and wearing a deep blue-green mantle over a red dress. She holds the infant Jesus on her lap. To her left, a bishop in ornate vestments with a tall mitre holds a golden crosier. To her right, a figure in full plate armor holds a red and white banner (Swiss cross). The background shows a large arched window with a blue sky. The composition is symmetrical and formal.
theological_description: The Solothurn Madonna presents the enthroned Virgin and Child flanked by the patron saints of the Swiss city of Solothurn. The arrangement is a sacra conversazione — a holy conversation across time in which saints from different eras attend the Madonna. Mary's crown identifies her as Queen of Heaven, a title rooted in Revelation's 'woman clothed with the sun, with the moon under her feet and a crown of twelve stars on her head' (Revelation 12:1). The bishop represents the teaching and pastoral authority of the Church, exercising the commission Christ gave to 'feed my sheep' (John 21:17). The armored saint embodies the Church Militant — Paul's exhortation to 'put on the full armor of God' (Ephesians 6:11). At the center of all earthly and heavenly authority sits the child Christ, 'for in him all the fullness of the Deity lives in bodily form' (Colossians 2:9).

scripture_refs.reason: The crowned woman — the vision behind Mary's title as Queen of Heaven
Put on the full armor of God — the soldier saint embodying spiritual warfare
Feed my sheep — the bishop's pastoral authority derived from Christ's commission
The fullness of deity in bodily form — the infant Christ at the center of the composition
